title: DSP Component Instances
text: DSP Component Instances DSP component instances differ from those of other components in that they are split—a non-persistent part runs on the server, and a persistent part runs on the browser. Communication between the two parts is asynchronous, via HTTP requests and responses over the internet. Uniface automatically handles communication and synchronization. Figure 1. Logical DSP Instance For security purposes, some definitions and logic are available only to the server-part of the DSP instance. Some others are only needed on the browser to improve the responsiveness of the application. The layout is maintained on the client, syntax checking is done both on the client and the server, and access to the back-end is only available to the server. Triggers are fired by the widgets on the client but can have a server- or a client-side implementation. Operations are explicitly activated by client- or server-side code and also have either a client or server implementation. Client logic is coded using JavaScript, whereas server logic is coded using Uniface ProcScript. Client Side of a DSP Instance The client-side of the DSP instance runs in the browser and is persistent. It remains active in the browser until the browser is closed, or until it is programmatically deleted. It can submit requests for server-side operations and triggers, or execute requests for client-side operations and triggers. It is possible to have many DSP instances running in a single web page on the browser, even if they are not all visible. For example, if you have a web page that is designed with a tab-like interface, clicking on a tab can display a DSP instance and hide the previous instance. DSP instances can be nested by means of DSP container widgets. Server Side of a DSP Instance The server-side of the DSP instance runs in the Uniface Server and is not persistent. It is instantiated by the Uniface Web Application Server each time it receives a request to activate a server-side trigger or operation on the instance. After the instance has generated a response, it is destroyed. It can execute triggers and operations in response to requests from the client. When it sends a response, it can include an instruction to activate a client-side operation or trigger. To recreate the DSP instance and associate it with the DSP instance on the client, the component name, component instance name, and state information must be included in each HTTP request sent from the browser. This is automatically managed by Uniface. Server side state needs to be explicitly made persistent by the developer, for example, using a database or other mechanism. For more information, see State Management Mechanisms . Communication The client side of the DSP instance is disconnected from the server process, so every action requiring a server-side operation or trigger to be executed, results in an explicit request being sent to the server. The server side generates a response, after which the connection is lost again. This is the nature of the web. Communication between the client and server side of the DSP instance is asynchronous. In the browser, each request to activate a trigger or operation is added to the request queue, which is responsible for dispatching the request, either to the server or to the client side of the DSP instance. For more information, see Client-Server Communication in DSPs . Life Cycle When the Uniface Web Application Server (WASV) receives a request from the browser for a new DSP instance: The Uniface Server uses the DSP definition in the repository to create (or recreate) the DSP instance on the server. The requested trigger or operation is activated, server-side business logic is executed, and a response is constructed. The WASV sends the response and destroys the server-side DSP instance. When the browser receives the response, it merges it with the client-side instance (or creates the client-side instance if it did not yet exist), and renders it on the screen. The client-side of the DSP instance is destroyed when it is programmatically deleted or when the browser is closed. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
